reviews.review_text: Somehow managed to play this game for the first time 28 years after it's release and I went in totally blind. Nostalgia was not a factor for me here, but this relic is a legend. This game is a sci-fi TRIP, man. Playing an unfamiliar game this old feels a bit like archeology, but with the promise of a rewarding find. I can see this game's influence on so many other titles.

Half-Life and its incredible demo Uplink prove that, even after all these years, they are not museum pieces, but masterpieces that still play like absolute beasts today. One of the game’s most groundbreaking aspects for its time was its seamless storytelling that never takes control away from the player, alongside the HECU military AI that still outclasses many modern FPS games. Soldiers flanking you from behind and flushing you out of cover with grenades was absolutely insane for that era.

From the iconic crowbar sound to the experimental Gauss Gun, the weapon variety is fantastic. The HEV Suit perfectly reinforces the atmosphere, while the physics puzzles are paced incredibly well throughout the game. The multiplayer side, HL:DM, is pure reflex and speed; with bunny hop mechanics, it still captures the chaotic yet fair competitive spirit of old-school FPS games. And thanks to the mod support that gave birth to giants like Counter-Strike, the replayability is practically endless.

My only real criticism is Xen. After the near-perfect atmosphere of Black Mesa, the low-gravity platforming sections slow the game’s momentum down and can feel frustrating in first person.

Even with that flaw, Half-Life — together with the short but impactful Uplink — remains one of the most important and rock-solid turning points in gaming history.

One of the best fps games ever made but god those last few chapters are just not fun. This game is at its best when you're gunning down armed militants. That's most of the game so it clearly knows this.
Half-Life is one of the best games ever made! It is ahead of its time, great story, and more!

When this game was first released in 1998, I must have played it at least 5 times through in the first couple of months. Today, we are so accustomed to FPS games that give total immersion with storyline and world building, but before 1998, none of these things had been refined. Then Half Life came along, and that opening tram ride with no action, just a 5 minute ride through the world, not to mention the inciting incident in the test chamber, absolutely blew up my expectations of what an FPS could deliver. 

I popped the game open yesterday, and while the technicals of the game are rough around the edges due to it being a nearly 30 year build of a game, the craftsmanship of this game are still absolutely apparent. A total masterpiece
Sick game, it's like a chiller doom, with more platforming sessions, i had a ton of fun

I was very excited to finally play such an important piece of videogame history, but despite its undeniable influence and a number of cool ideas, I just can't get over the things that aged like milk.
The movement in this game is so infuriating. The level design flip flops between brilliant and horrible. The audio is torture. There are so many little things that make this game *feel* old.

I can't lie: I did not have a good time playing this.

Revisiting this masterpiece after years. I don't think I need to emphasize just how great this game is.
For those that are unable to play games from this era due to low poly graphics, try out black mesa instead.

Classic valve game but gameplay and story don't translate or hold up very well in the modern age. Its like your grandpa you like and respect him BUT don't always wanna spend time with him or sit through his stories of the "good" old days.

A classic valve masterpiece no bad games came in the height of '98, nothing goes wrong when it's from valve! The game was nice and short, but not too short~ took me roughly 4 days to a week to beat. Though it doesn't have a lot of replay value or things to do after you beat it (I say as I haven't touched multiplayer at all)

feel and general atmosphere: 8/10

times I got softlocked in a quicksave death loop and had to cheat to get out of it: like 4 or 5
overall rating 9/10
(total analytic rating 31/40 or 29/30)

What is there to say about Half-Life that hasn’t been said before? It’s a classic in the FPS genre that’s still a joy to play. I’m surprised I haven’t played through the campaign until now since I have hours in Valve’s other games from this era (including Ricochet) 

The boss battles in the last section of the game were lackluster, but the set pieces leading up to those moments were epic. I guess ‘On The Rails’ and Interloper were the weakest parts of the game; I actually liked on the rails, but interloper and the rest of the alien levels should have been worked on longer. I liked their version of what an alien universe would look like, but I don’t like the level design. Another thing I found annoying is the alien grunt enemies, I despise their homing bug projectiles. 

There isn’t really a story but more of one than earlier FPS games. Not really a downside, just what it is. It can be funny in a dark way though, which is a staple in their games 

Overall really good. The scientists are my favorite part of the game.

Just finished the game.
i was genuily surprised when i played it, it was made in 1993 and looks like a game from the early (very early) 2000s (or late 90s).
Although i couldnt understand the story too much since the sound quality isnt the best, i recommend playing this.
9/10 game.
Also, cough cough virus guy makes me pee the bed at night. This is not a laughing matter.

I was introduced to Valve over a decade ago with a copy of Half Life 2 given by a friend, but only now have I finished playing the one that started it all. It was fun to see the seeds of various design practices that would evolve to define Valve and game/narrative design as a whole. In spite of all the jank and outdated graphics, the gameplay holds up remarkably well for a nearly 30 year old game and the presentation is very charming. I wasn't there when this game was released so I'll never truly know how mindblowing any of this was, but that I barely felt any issues in gameplay in my context of modern gaming could be a testament to this game's brilliance. Overall, I loved my time with it.

Except Xen. ♥♥♥♥ Xen.

Half-Life mogs Half-Life 2
Everyone always talks about the memes and the funny dialogue, but no one ever mentions how genuinely scary this game was. Every encounter feels intense and dangerous. This game truly lives up to its title — you feel half alive the entire time. There’s a constant doomed, oppressive atmosphere.
Enemies can one-shot you, everywhere you go feels like total chaos, and every NPC has unique AI that makes firefights incredibly tense. This is the best Valve game, period.
The Xen chapter is overhated, and Nihilanth is a peak final boss. This game in general has kino boss fights. The platforming might filter some people, but it’s good game design.
100/10

Firstly, and most crucially: The game is a relic of its time. I think every issue I had with the game—unclear paths, bugs, and janky controls—is just a consequence of the game being a revolutionary title from 1998.
However, the “unclear paths” point brings me to my biggest takeaway from this game’s design: it forces you to be immersed in order to succeed. You need to think for yourself, try everything, and let people live if you want to receive their assistance. You aren’t just told exactly what to do at every moment and handheld down a path, like so many games do today. You need to figure out how to escape Black Mesa on your own. To that end, the game’s demanding of your immersion and concentration is probably my favorite thing about it.
I looked up what to do several times, but in my opinion, every time I didn’t know where to go was just me being an idiot, except for two different occasions: first, me not obtaining a pistol at the start of Waste Processing because I didn’t follow the guard, leading me to do the whole chapter without it before googling the pistol’s location and restarting the chapter; and second, the flooding of the Lambda Core bugging so that it didn’t fill all the way because I turned the two valves in quick succession. Because of those two incidents, the only two changes I would make to this game are to have the guard die to the headcrabs he’s fighting, and to fix that bug with the flooding.
Sure, the game sometimes aggravated me or bored me, but I cannot in good faith say that any other part of this game could be changed without worsening the final product overall. The game’s goal is to immerse you into its world and make you feel the exact amounts of fear, tension, and power that Freeman himself would feel; it accomplishes that pretty much flawlessly. After finally beating Half-Life for the first time, I get why it’s a legendary classic.
I would highly recommend this game even today. The story telling without cutscenes although basic in many ways is effective even by todays standards.

game would be much better if it took place in brookhaven national labs or someplace in boston. Who the ♥♥♥♥ cares about los alamos national labs?! (fun fact one of my graduate student friends works there now).

Somtimes you have to wiggle the mouse and spam crouch while jumping to get over basic obsticles, as well as there being many corners with invisible walls to get stuck on. This is the first FPS game I have ever had so much fun playing. Okay, so maybe it is the only FPS game I have ever played, but that doesn't make it worse! While I've heard it gets worse in later chapters, the game allows the player to employ special tactics to a situation. You can use stealth to bomb the enemies with precise timing, you can be aggressive when suit power is high. There is nothing I have ever played that matches the feeling of failing with my aggressive approach and coming up with a stealthy one! Truly a great game for almost anyone into the genre.

It's great. Honestly my unbridled love for Abiotic Factor played a heavy hand in me returning to Half Life and finally finishing it. For its time: you can tell how rich this game was. Here are some highlights if you're somehow on the fence about Valve's own darling:

[list]
    [*]Controls well enough. Easy to use quick save/load, aiming is responsive, and platform is mostly manageable. Keeping always-run on does lead to some scenarios where you feel like you're landing on ice, which makes the game's platforming a challenge, but it's so fast to get back into the action it's hardly noticeable.
    [*]The deaths in this game are so hilariously quick and violent that it's really dang funny. Coupled with the iconic Half Life sounds I mostly chuckled at my demise than bemoaned it.
    [*]The biggest pull of this game, to this day, is the storytelling. If you didn't know: this title is credited as important for gaming history for being one of the first to really put its foot forward into storytelling and voiced dialogue before a lot of its competition. The opening chapter is still my favorite, with its multitude of NPCs, their jobs, lives, and workplace worries.
    [*]If I had to list a major complaint: it would be level design / player guidance. We're in an age of YouTube longplays with no commentary, so it's easy to find out what you're just not seeing, but I wish I didn't have to use one at all. Sometimes you just don't see the beam you're supposed to jump onto or the proper conveyor belt sequence. If anything this is a plea to those stuck: just pull up a video and find out. You lose nothing from jumping around a hallway wondering why you can't proceed.
    [*]I would say the human soldiers in this title also have crazy MLG-esque skills when they decide to throw a grenade every other bullet. The amount of times I shot, retreated to a knee-high pipe... only to find a grenade bounce expertly off a wall and into my hiding spot: was often.
[/list]

If you wanted to see why Abiotic Factor was created as a love letter to Half Life: it's never a bad time to pick this up. It's like a 5~ hour game.

Ignore time played i've played all the way through this about a dozen times just never on this platform. Almost three decades later and this is still a game I find myself comparing more recent FPS titles and frequently they come up short. During the 90s I played everything in the subgenre that I could and loved a lot of them but this was on a whole other level. This feels like you are playing A8 80s Sci fi action horror flick. And I'm not just saying that because of the obvious aliens influence. It doesn't hold your hand and over explain nor does ever really take away control from you. You can move during cutscenes and look at what you feel like. They opt for the Star Wars model where everyone basically knows the world so no one just expose it endlessly and it works so well. This technically has escort missions and it's one of the only times I've ever encountered where those are not actually frustrating. You tell the NPC when follow you and when to stay put so you clear an area of enemies and then the NPC through it. Weapons are fun well rounded and deeply memorable. The level of realism is impressive for something depicting an alien invasion. When you find health ammo and guns It's always in areas that make sense. The gameplay is quite varied for an FPS and without it ever feeling like it just becomes a different game. I acknowledge that there is at least one chunk that is for those of us who also love platformers. The tutorial is intuitive and skippable though I'd recommend playing through at least once but then you don't have to no matter how many times you come back to this later. It's cinematic from the very start. Enemy designs and variety is Great not to mention the AI especially for squads which raised the bar. obviously by today's standards stuff like graphics and audio is not as impressive anymore but it has aged much better then stuff that is only half as old as this in some cases . I ran it briefly to test and it was just as smooth as back when it was brand new . It's great the way that every so often you will face a new problem to solve as a player and it really has you thinking instead of just running around shooting which I appreciate is in other FPS games but he would have felt old here . This feels so carefully crafted every step of the way nothing is left to chance and have hardly ever run into any bugs or glitches and certainly never anything major . Strongly recommended
PEAK from the moment i first hit new game to the end, PEAK for a game made in 1998, great story really fun gameplay it cant get any better than this

I can see why this is considered a classic and how it influenced so many future linear story-driven FPS games. The level design and storytelling feel remarkably modern, with both working in unison, unlike many games from the same era. The sound design and environmental detail are excellent.

There is so much enemy and environmental variety that it's hard to get bored. The levels feature numerous interactive objects and mechanisms that feel natural within the setting of a research facility, helping the world feel believable and lived-in.

I especially appreciated the minimal hand-holding, both in gameplay and story exposition. The game respects the player's intelligence and allows them to become fully immersed in the experience, which is definitely a breath of fresh air compared to much of modern gaming.

It ends on an intriguing cliffhanger. On to Half-Life 2!
This game may be old, but its story, game play, and puzzles are still great. The graphics should use some work...But half life source does NOT fix this due to its bugs.
What an absolutely awesome game full of memorable setpieces, characters and surprisingly out of the box puzzles, until it starts sucking with Xen at the end

As the predecessor to the legendary Half-Life 2, Half-Life absolutely stands on its own merits of intense combat, imaginative level design, and abundant atmosphere--all in service of an ever-unravelling mystery that goes far deeper than you'd ever expect.

What struck me the most was that Half-Life offers no hand-holding. Utterly unconcerned with accessibility, this game offers a stark contrast to modern games. There is no tutorial, no objective markers or menus, and the limited instructions that are given, are given verbally and said only once. You'd better be listening too, because this game doesn't even have an option for subtitles. While I do think that accessibility is a good thing, its absence here elevates the player's sense of adventure and hammers home the narrative that Gordon Freeman really is on his own.

Adding to its atmosphere and sense of adventure are the distinct and varied levels. From open and exposed to tight and claustrophobic, not a single level feels repetitive. Combined with an array of weapons to choose from and a cast of enemies with different abilities, Half-Life constantly builds on its own mechanics in a satisfying progression. Don't be fooled by the easy levels at the beginning--this game gets hard. 

The one thing I don't like about this game is the platforming. The player is on skates, and what should be easy jumps can be very frustrating at times. It's usually fine, but I don't like feeling like I have to save after every jump just in case I fail the next one. 

Obviously, I do recommend this game. I had a blast putting together the story and making my way through the hostile Black Mesa Complex. I want to finish my review by saying that I personally love games that let the player play at their own pace. Half-Life is such a game. If you know what you're doing, then you're free to run-and-gun your way to the end. But, if you want to take your time and check your blind-spots, the game often rewards you with ammo and powerpacks. 

Half-Life is loved by fans to this day because it was made with love. I played it to get some gamer-cred, but I leave it with some very fond memories.

I'm a pretty young person but I grew up playing shooters, it's my favourite genre and as of late I've been doing a deep dive into other old titles. Even after playing through a bunch of those gems, none of them even come close to Half Life. The gunplay feels amazing, the weapon and enemy variety always leaves room for surprises and the levels you traverse all offer different challenges for you to overcome. 

One of the most unique thing about this game though is that it really emphasises storytelling. Usually classic shooters have a very basic plot that purely serves to connect the levels, and while there's nothing wrong with prioritising gameplay over story Half Life actually makes you feel like you're making progress through the facility and uncovering its secrets. It still blows me away replaying it years later.

I will forever owe Half Life 1 and 2 for fostering my love for video games, and while I am definitely biased I 100% recommend this game to anyone, both fans of classic shooters and people looking to get into the genre.

Writing this after having finished my third playthrough! |This game is very fun, even if it's begun showing its age. The attempts at cinematography along with the Quake-like gameplay are endearing if anything else. You can actually feel how unfinished Xen is once you play it; I didn't even bother finishing it on my two final playthroughs!
Overall this game is a must play if you like FPS games, not only because it's a great game overall, but also because it's a good history lesson for the genre!
One of the best and revolutionary games of all time, a must play if you like FPS.

Its quite an old game, but for people that want to fit the aesthetic of 1999? Take this.

It uses a very old version of the Source engine, yeah the one that most Valve games are built around. Here, most physics objects... Dont exist. But sometimes, they do! Unrotatable, but movable. Destructable, exploding, etc. 
This game, compared to its Sequel, is very hard. Its much longer than Half-Life 2, and you see a lot of other stuff - inside Black Mesa's main labs, inside wacky tunnels, outside Black Mesa, inside Zen... And, also, theres a lot of other enemies and weapons in this game, rather than in the second game.

So yeah, buy this game. I think it also gives you Half Life Deathmatch: Source for free after you buy it, so you can ALSO get one of the most needed content for Garrys Mod.

Gameplay is really fun with a great lore and great design. I love the enemy design aswell and the weapons are well thought out. This is a love letter to all who love classic shooters.

Absolutely worth playing, Especially if you're like me and had somehow managed to avoid spoilers for this series (yes, I intend to play the next installments as well). Physics are a bit janky at times (though that is to be expected for a game this old) and it's actually quite difficult even on lower challenge settings (I did my playthrough on medium difficulty).

Don't make the mistake I did: play the tutorial shooting range before you start the story.

Still a shining diamond of a game all these years later. Half Life's got a great grasp of just letting the player breathe -- no ever-present tips, huge quest markers, or voiced protagonist muttering something about the puzzle every other minute. Its tricks and traps are all a dose of clever and a dose of 90's meanness (check your corners!), and its level design is still so striking. It's a game whose DNA you see everywhere, once you've played it, and for good reason. A little masterclass that every PC gamer should play at least once.
